metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Radio up to the Minute, a hobbyist textbook published in 1924 by John R. Nilson, an American radio engineer.
main_question:
Early in the history of radio, Marconi demonstrated that radiation from an antenna was directional in its effect, according to the shape of the aerial employed. It was this discovery which led in later years to the wonderful success of the direction finder or radio compass. It is well known, that a single vertical wire is, for its size the best radiator, [masked clause revising an implied expectation].

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause revising an implied expectation]:
answer_strings: but it has to be made so extremely long in order to obtain sufficient capacity that it is not a practical antenna for long wave or long distance work
but it must be so short in order to obtain sufficient capacity that it becomes impractical for long wave or long distance work
but with the aid of the Italian and British governments he increased the range of his apparatus until he had demonstrated that radio was a practical commercial possibility with unlimited scope
however, it was also found that this type of antenna did not efficiently cover all directions

however, its efficiency drops sharply when the frequency is from the quarter-wave resonance, and installations must account for ground conductivity and nearby structures, which can further reduce performance
passage: Early in the history of radio, Marconi demonstrated that radiation from an antenna was directional in its effect, according to the shape of the aerial employed. It was this discovery which led in later years to the wonderful success of the direction finder or radio compass. It is well known, that a single vertical wire is, for its size the best radiator, but it has to be made so extremely long in order to obtain sufficient capacity that it is not a practical antenna for long wave or long distance work.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Radio up to the Minute, a hobbyist textbook published in 1924 by John R. Nilson, an American radio engineer.
main_question:
The difference between the gravity primary cell, previously referred to, and the secondary or storage cell, is in the method of renewing the active material. While the primary cell is renewed by supplying new electrolyte and replacing the worn out zinc electrode with a new one, dry cells cannot be renewed. [masked sentence revising an implied expectation] While the cell is supplying emf., it is said to be "discharging" and when receiving a renewal of energy it is said to be "charging."

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence revising an implied expectation]:
answer_strings: In the storage battery, however, the necessary chemical conditions of the plates is restored by the action of a current of electricity from some outer source, usually from a dynamo.
In the storage battery, however, the necessary chemical conditions of the plates are depleted by the action of a current of electricity from some outer source, usually from a dynamo.

If, however, five amperes is the rate mentioned on the normal discharge and charge rate of the cell, it should only be discharged at that rate and also recharged at that rate, which would give the normal usefulness as five amperes for eight hours.
However, when the plates are connected by a wire or some other conductor outside of the liquid, a current will flow in the conductor, as a chemical action takes place in the cell.
However, secondary cells can be recharged repeatedly, as they use reversible chemical reactions.
However, the secondary or storage cell can be recharged by applying an external current, thereby reversing the electrochemical reaction and restoring its original emf, thus extending its useful life.
passage: The difference between the gravity primary cell, previously referred to, and the secondary or storage cell, is in the method of renewing the active material. While the primary cell is renewed by supplying new electrolyte and replacing the worn out zinc electrode with a new one, dry cells cannot be renewed. In the storage battery, however, the necessary chemical conditions of the plates is restored by the action of a current of electricity from some outer source, usually from a dynamo. While the cell is supplying emf., it is said to be "discharging" and when receiving a renewal of energy it is said to be "charging."

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Radio up to the Minute, a hobbyist textbook published in 1924 by John R. Nilson, an American radio engineer.
main_question:
In some cases the studio is on the ground floor of a building and the power and control rooms are located on the roof directly under the aerial wires. In most cases at least two studios are provided, one for large assemblies and a smaller one for individual speakers. The studio must be very carefully planned, [masked clause describing a cause or reason]. No matter how well the transmitter operates if the acoustic properties of the studio are bad the broadcasting will sound bad in the radio receivers.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing a cause or reason]:
answer_strings: for on it depends the success of a broadcasting station
for the average reader to think of ether as the conducting medium for radio waves
because it is where the program originates
because listeners judge broadcasts primarily on studio acoustics and clarity
passage: In some cases the studio is on the ground floor of a building and the power and control rooms are located on the roof directly under the aerial wires. In most cases at least two studios are provided, one for large assemblies and a smaller one for individual speakers. The studio must be very carefully planned, for on it depends the success of a broadcasting station. No matter how well the transmitter operates if the acoustic properties of the studio are bad the broadcasting will sound bad in the radio receivers.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Radio up to the Minute, a hobbyist textbook published in 1924 by John R. Nilson, an American radio engineer.
main_question:
Another very practicable type for certain work is the V antenna, consisting of two sets of horizontal wires supported by three masts or towers, so that the horizontal portions form an angle or V. The directional effect of an inverted L or V type is greater than a T. There is a greater amount of energy sent in the direction in which the angle of the L points than in the opposite. With the T type the effect is more undirectional, [masked clause acknowledging a countervailing fact].

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause acknowledging a countervailing fact]:
answer_strings: although more energy is sent in the parallel direction of the horizontal wires, than at right angles
although less energy is sent in the parallel direction of the horizontal wires, than at right angles
although ether waves may be of different form to water waves
although it will be found that later, in discussing undamped wave receivers, very slight modifications of the damped wave apparatus will give one method of receiving undamped waves
although the energy radiated in all directions is not as concentrated
although the T type is less directional, it provides more uniform coverage across all angles globally
passage: Another very practicable type for certain work is the V antenna, consisting of two sets of horizontal wires supported by three masts or towers, so that the horizontal portions form an angle or V. The directional effect of an inverted L or V type is greater than a T. There is a greater amount of energy sent in the direction in which the angle of the L points than in the opposite. With the T type the effect is more undirectional, although more energy is sent in the parallel direction of the horizontal wires, than at right angles.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Radio up to the Minute, a hobbyist textbook published in 1924 by John R. Nilson, an American radio engineer.
main_question:
The same circuit utilized for charging lead cells may be employed for charging Edison cells. The charging source should have a voltage equal to 1.85 times the number of cells in series. Before starting to charge, open the covers of the compartment, [masked clause describing a condition or proviso]. See that the solution is at the proper level.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing a condition or proviso]:
answer_strings: if the battery is in one
unless the battery is in one
if the grid be charged negatively
if a piece of magnetite is dipped into iron or steel filings
provided that all cells are connected properly
if the battery is fully rested
passage: The same circuit utilized for charging lead cells may be employed for charging Edison cells. The charging source should have a voltage equal to 1.85 times the number of cells in series. Before starting to charge, open the covers of the compartment, if the battery is in one. See that the solution is at the proper level.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Radio up to the Minute, a hobbyist textbook published in 1924 by John R. Nilson, an American radio engineer.
main_question:
Therefore, when an electron, negatively charged, leaves an atom, there is then less negative electricity than positive in the atom; in this condition the atom is said to be positively charged. The atoms in matter are constantly in motion, and when they strike against one another electrons are jarred from an atom. This electron then moves about freely between the atoms. [masked sentence describing an inference or effect] The higher the temperature, the faster the atoms move and the more electrons given off.

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence describing an inference or effect]:
answer_strings: Heat has an effect upon this process.
Heat has no effect upon this process.
This effect of the electrons in the space is called the "space charge effect."
Thus both of the parts of the disrupted atom become carriers of electricity and contribute to the flow of current through the gas.
Hence, the material begins to conduct electricity.
Consequently, the free electrons increase the material's conductivity.
passage: Therefore, when an electron, negatively charged, leaves an atom, there is then less negative electricity than positive in the atom; in this condition the atom is said to be positively charged. The atoms in matter are constantly in motion, and when they strike against one another electrons are jarred from an atom. This electron then moves about freely between the atoms. Heat has an effect upon this process. The higher the temperature, the faster the atoms move and the more electrons given off.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Radio up to the Minute, a hobbyist textbook published in 1924 by John R. Nilson, an American radio engineer.
main_question:
The electric field intensity, due to the presence of negative electrons in the space between the filament and plate, may at last equal and neutralize that due to the positive potential of the plate so that there is no force acting on the electrons near the filament. This effect of the electrons in the space is called the "space charge effect." It must not be supposed that the space charge effect is caused by the same electrons all the time. Electrons near the plate are constantly entering it, but new electrons emitted by the filament are entering the space, [masked clause describing an inference or effect]. After the temperature of the filament has reached the point where the effect of the electrons present in the space between filament and plate neutralizes the effect of the plate voltage, any further increase of the filament temperature is unable to cause an increase in the current.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing an inference or effect]:
answer_strings: so that the total number between filament and plate remains constant at a given temperature
so that the total number between filament and plate fluctuates despite the temperature
this then creates an equilibrium between electron emission and absorption
then the space charge gradually increases, counterbalancing the plate's field and reducing electron acceleration near filament
passage: The electric field intensity, due to the presence of negative electrons in the space between the filament and plate, may at last equal and neutralize that due to the positive potential of the plate so that there is no force acting on the electrons near the filament. This effect of the electrons in the space is called the "space charge effect." It must not be supposed that the space charge effect is caused by the same electrons all the time. Electrons near the plate are constantly entering it, but new electrons emitted by the filament are entering the space, so that the total number between filament and plate remains constant at a given temperature. After the temperature of the filament has reached the point where the effect of the electrons present in the space between filament and plate neutralizes the effect of the plate voltage, any further increase of the filament temperature is unable to cause an increase in the current.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Radio up to the Minute, a hobbyist textbook published in 1924 by John R. Nilson, an American radio engineer.
main_question:
The two types of detectors which act as rectifiers are, namely, the crystal detector and the vacuum tube detector. When a crystal detector is employed it is sometimes necessary to have some way of testing whether or not the detector is in adjustment or is "set." [masked sentence describing a cause or reason]

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence describing a cause or reason]:
answer_strings: For this purpose a test buzzer is usually fitted to a crystal receiver.
For this purpose a test buzzer is usually disconnected from a crystal receiver.
Because detector sensitivity varies, testing ensures accurate rectification and proper alignment.
passage: The two types of detectors which act as rectifiers are, namely, the crystal detector and the vacuum tube detector. When a crystal detector is employed it is sometimes necessary to have some way of testing whether or not the detector is in adjustment or is "set." For this purpose a test buzzer is usually fitted to a crystal receiver.
